steps.title: Identify what the underlined portion does
steps.content: Camillo says his presence might be “some allay” to the king’s sorrows, then immediately adds “or I o’erween [presume] to think so.” That parenthetical pulls back the claim — he’s acknowledging he merely assumes he can help, not that he knows it.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — It establishes Camillo's hope that Polixenes will be comforted knowing that Camillo isn't departing at his own discretion.: The underlined portion is about Camillo’s uncertainty over helping the Sicilian king, not about reassuring Polixenes. ✗
  • Choice B — It suggests that Camillo feels compelled to persuade Polixenes that Camillo's decision to leave the court is justified.: Camillo isn’t building a persuasive case; he’s simply noting a hope and then qualifying it. ✗
  • Choice C — It conveys Camillo's recognition that one benefit his presence may afford the king of Sicily is merely speculative.: Exactly — “I presume to think so” flags the benefit as an assumption, not a certainty. ✓
  • Choice D — It bolsters the idea that Camillo's primary motivation to return home is his concern for the king of Sicily's well-being in his absence.: The underlined portion actually weakens rather than bolsters the claim about the king’s well-being by marking it as presumed. ✗
That matches Choice C — It conveys Camillo's recognition that one benefit his presence may afford the king of Sicily is merely speculative..
